1: perēṣ lōʾ-niwttar bāh — the wall is complete and ‘no breach remained in it’ — perēṣ (breach, gap, rupture) is the technical term for wall-integrity. The root prṣ can mean to burst through, to spread/break out (2 Samuel 5:20: YHWH ‘burst out’ against the Philistines). In Ezekiel 22:30, YHWH sought someone to stand in the perēṣ (breach) before him; Nehemiah has achieved what the prophet described — the breach is filled. However, ûdĕlātôt lōʾ hĕʿĕmadtî (I had not set up doors in the gates) — the wall is continuous but incomplete; the gates cannot yet be locked. This detail explains why the enemies acted quickly.
2: wayyiššĕlĕḥû (they sent) + nôʿădāh (let us meet by appointment) — the root yāʿad (to set a time, meet by appointment) is the verb for assembly and for trysts; in the wilderness narrative, the ‘tent of meeting’ (ʾōhel môʿēd) uses the same root. The meeting proposed in the plain of Ono has the grammatical form of collegial consultation but Nehemiah reads its purpose differently: lôʾ-ṭôḇāh hēmmāh ḥōšĕḇîm laʿăśôt lî (‘they were planning to do me harm’) — the verb ḥšb (to think/plan) combined with rāʿāh (evil) is the conspiracy vocabulary used throughout the wisdom literature.
3: melaʾkāh gĕdôlāh ʾănî ʿōśeh — ‘I am engaged in a great work.’ Melaʾkāh (work, task, commissioned project) is the same word used for the tabernacle construction (Exodus 36:2,4,5,6,7,8) and the temple-building (1 Kings 7:14; 1 Chronicles 29:1); by using this term, Nehemiah implicitly places his wall-building in the category of sacred construction. The rhetorical question lāmmāh tiŝbōt hammĕlaʾkāh wĕyāradtî ʾălêhem (‘why should the work stop while I come down to you?’) uses yārad (to go down/descend) for the proposed journey; the verb is also the metaphor for abandoning one’s elevated position or calling. Nehemiah will not ‘come down’ — physically or spiritually — from the sacred task.
4: ʾarbaʿ pĕʿāmîm kaddāḇār hazzeh — ‘four times like this same thing’ — the iterative repetition (four times the same message, four times the same reply) is a narrative technique in the Hebrew prose style for showing persistent pressure and equally persistent refusal. The word pĕʿāmîm (times, occasions) is the standard word for repeated actions (cf. Genesis 43:10: ‘we could have gone there twice by now’). The sameness of the answer (kaddāḇār hazzeh, ‘like this word’) emphasizes Nehemiah’s consistent refusal — four iterations without wavering.
5: sefer pātûaḥ — an ‘open/unsealed letter’ was a deliberate public shaming tactic. In the ancient Near East, letters were sealed with clay tablets, wax, or cord-and-clay to ensure confidentiality and authenticate the sender. An unsealed letter was accessible to anyone who handled it along the way — porters, gatekeepers, bystanders. Sending an unsealed letter with damaging accusations was a calculated humiliation: it made the contents public knowledge before Nehemiah could respond. The aide (naʿărô, his servant/boy) was Sanballat’s junior envoy, not a senior official — another slight in the mode of delivery.
6: timrōd (you are planning to rebel) — root mrd (to rebel, revolt), the verb for political insurrection (Genesis 14:4: ‘the kings rebelled’; Joshua 22:16-19: the confrontation about the altar as potential rebellion). In the Persian administrative context, the accusation of mĕrîdāh (rebellion) was extremely serious — it could justify military intervention and execution. Artaxerxes had already halted the Jerusalem building project due to just such an accusation in Ezra 4:12-16. The letter is not merely diplomatic pressure but a legal threat: we have grounds to report you to the king as a traitor.
7: ʾăšer-melaḵîm bîhûdāh — ‘there is a king in Judah’ — the accusation adds a prophetic dimension: Nehemiah has appointed prophets to proclaim his kingship. The verb qārāʾ (to proclaim/call out) is the standard word for prophetic proclamation. The double accusation — you plan to revolt, and you have hired prophets to legitimize it — is a comprehensive treason charge. wĕʿattāh (and now) is the consequence-clause, standard in ANE political correspondence: ‘given all this, come now and let us discuss it together.’ The invitation is framed as crisis management, but it is a trap.
8: lōʾ-nihĕyu haddĕḇārîm hāʾēlleh kāʾăšer ʾattāh ʾômēr — ‘none of these things happened as you say’ — a flat denial using the perfect tense (nihĕyu, ‘were not’/‘did not happen’), not a qualified defense. millib-bĕkā ʾattāh bādēʾm — ‘from your heart you are fabricating/inventing them’ — the verb bādāh (to invent, make up) appears only a few times in the OT (1 Kings 12:33: Jeroboam ‘invented a festival from his own heart’); the attribution millib-bĕkā (from your heart) locates the fabrication entirely in Sanballat’s own imagination, not in any evidence. The accusation is pure invention.
9: kî kullām mĕyarʾîm ʾôtānû — ‘for they were all trying to frighten us’ — the Piel participle of yrʾ (to fear) in the causative sense (to make fear, to frighten). yirpenû yĕdêhem — ‘so our hands would go limp’ — the verb rāpāh (to be slack, weak, feeble; cf. Hebrews 12:12: ‘lift up your drooping hands’) in the Qal imperfect; slack hands is the Hebrew idiom for demoralization and abandonment of a task. The countermeasure is the arrow-prayer: ḥazzēq ʾeṯ-yādāy (‘strengthen my hands’) — the Piel imperative of ḥzq, requesting the direct opposite of what the enemy intended. The prayer is five words in Hebrew — the entire verse’s theology in a flash-petition.
10: wĕhûʾ ʿāṣûr — ‘and he was confined/homebound’ — the Qal passive of ʿṣr (to restrain, detain, be confined). The same root is used for imprisoned officials (Genesis 39:20: Joseph imprisoned) and for ritual confinement (Leviticus 13:5: the priest confines the person with skin disease). Shemaiah’s confinement could signal ritual purity (making him seem more prophetically credible), claimed illness (giving the meeting an urgency), or staged drama (the private sanctuary meeting was more compelling if the prophet was already in a heightened state). The word ʿāṣûr alone does not tell us which.
11: ûmî kāmônî ʾăšer-yāḇôʾ ʾel-hahêkāl wāḥāy — ‘and who, like me, would enter the temple and live?’ — the issue is priestly purity law: entering the sanctuary interior was restricted to priests; for a layman to enter was to die (Numbers 18:7: ‘the outsider who comes near shall be put to death’). Nehemiah was not a priest; entering the sanctuary (hêkāl, the nave/main hall, not merely the court) to hide would have been a capital offense under priestly law. The argument has two levels: (1) it is undignified for a leader to flee; (2) the proposed refuge would itself be a sin. The double refusal makes flight impossible on both honor and legal grounds.
12: wĕhinnēh lōʾ ʾĕlōhîm šĕlāḥô — ‘and behold, God had not sent him’ — the discernment formula for identifying false prophecy. The criterion YHWH/God had not sent him is the definitive test in Jeremiah 23:21 (‘I did not send these prophets, yet they ran’), Jeremiah 27:15; Nehemiah 28:15; Ezekiel 13:6. kî hattôbîyāh wĕsanballat śĕḵārûhû (‘because Tobiah and Sanballat had hired him’) — the Qal perfect of śkr (to hire) reveals the financial mechanism behind the false prophecy. A hired prophet (nāḇîʾ śāḵîr) is categorically delegitimized — the mercenary motive corrupts the message entirely (cf. Micah 3:11: prophets who divine for money).
13: lĕmaʿan ʾîrāʾ wĕʾeʿĕśeh-kēn wĕḥāṭāʾtî — ‘so that I would be afraid and act accordingly, and so sin’ — the causal chain: fear → action → sin. The verb ḥāṭāʾ (to sin, miss the mark) identifies the proposed action (entering the sanctuary) as a formal covenant violation, not merely a social embarrassment. wĕhāyāh lāhem lĕšēm rāʿ (so that they could give me a bad reputation) — the motive of reputation-damage through inducing a specific sin that could then be publicly reported. The enemies’ tactic is sophisticated: use a paid prophet to create a situation where Nehemiah either commits a sin (entering the sanctuary = priestly privilege) or is seen as a coward (fleeing to it).
14: zĕḵārāh ʾĕlōhay lĕṭôḇîyāh ûlĕsanballaṭ — the second ‘remember me’ prayer (cf. Nehemiah 5:19), but now inverted: remember them for judgment. The word zĕḵārāh is the same imperative as 5:19 but directed against enemies rather than on behalf of Nehemiah; the prayer form is the same, the content is imprecatory. nôʿadyāh hanĕḇîʾāh (the prophetess Noadiah) — a female prophet among the opposition, unique in the Nehemiah narrative; the mention of a female prophet attempting to frighten Nehemiah is a rare glimpse of women in prophetic roles in the post-exilic community, even in opposition.
15: bĕʿeśrîm waḥămiššāh lĕʾĕlûl — the precise dating (25th of Elul, the 6th month) combined with the ḥămiššîm ûšĕnayim yôm (52 days) is one of Nehemiah’s most specific historical claims. Elul = August-September. The 52-day duration (from beginning of construction to completion of the wall — though gaps and gates remained) is extraordinarily fast; it was subsequently noted by Josephus as remarkable. The specificity of the date serves both as a historical record and as evidence for divine assistance — what took years for other projects was accomplished in less than two months.
16: wayyippĕlû mĕʾōd bĕʿênêhem — literally ‘they fell greatly in their eyes’ — the idiom of falling in one’s own eyes (ʿênêhem, their eyes) means self-devaluation or profound shame. The enemies’ confidence (bĕṭaḥôn) collapsed because they recognized the achievement as divine rather than merely human: wayyēdĕʿû kî mēʾet ʾĕlōhênû neʿĕśĕtāh hammĕlaʾkāh hazzōʾt (‘for they knew that this work had been accomplished with the help of our God’). The Niphal neʿĕśĕtāh (was accomplished/was done) has a passive-divine nuance: the work was done, implying it was done through/by them but ultimately by God. The enemies’ recognition of divine agency is an indirect acknowledgment of YHWH’s power.
17: wûḇayyāmîm hāhēm — ‘also during that period’ — the temporal marker indicates a concurrent development alongside the wall-building. The ‘nobles of Judah’ (ḥōrê yĕhûdāh) — the freemen/aristocrats of the community — were sending numerous letters to Tobiah and receiving replies. The verb hōlĕkôt (going, coming and going) suggests an ongoing correspondence, not a single exchange. The ḥōrîm (freemen, nobles) are distinct from the śĕgānîm (officials) — this is the upper crust of Judean society maintaining diplomatic ties with the external opponent.
18: kî-raḇbîm bîhûdāh baʿălê šĕḇûʿô — ‘for many in Judah were bound to him by oath’ — the baʿălê šĕḇûʿô (masters/owners of his oath) is an unusual phrase; it means ‘those who had taken an oath with him,’ i.e., those who were parties to a binding agreement with Tobiah. The double marriage-connection (Tobiah = son-in-law of Shecaniah son of Arah; his son Jehohanan = married daughter of Meshullam son of Berechiah) reveals why the aristocratic community maintained ties with the opposition: Tobiah was family to two of the most prominent Judean families.
19: ṭôḇōtāyw — ‘his good deeds’ — the word ṭôḇōtāyw (his acts of good/his benefits) from ṭôḇ has an ironic weight in this context: the same root (ṭôḇāh) appears in Nehemiah’s ‘remember me for good’ prayers (Nehemiah 5:19; Nehemiah 13:31). The nobles were presenting Tobiah’s ṭôḇôt before Nehemiah, while Nehemiah prayed for YHWH’s ṭôḇāh (favor) on himself — the same word in opposing directions. wĕdĕḇāray hāyû môṣîʾîm lô (and my words were being reported to him) — the Hiphil participle of yṣʾ (to bring out, report back); Tobiah had an intelligence pipeline directly from Nehemiah’s circle through the nobles who functioned as unwitting spies.
